story:
On flat, wooded farmland where neighbors live far apart, an old man buries his wife and returns to a house that feels like it’s already forgotten him. His children don’t ride back with him; the empty passenger seat and the pigeons they once fed together are all that keep him company. He climbs to the attic in his funeral suit to reset the routine—scraping guano, pouring seed, trying to prove he can manage alone. As the birds whirl in from the August sky, two land on the open trapdoor and knock it against his shoulder as he starts down the ladder. He plunges backward into the attic floorboards, hits his head, and comes to hours later with dried blood sealing his eyes.

The roof cage flap is banging, and he knows he made a fatal mistake: he left it open all night. In the attic he finds blood, feathers, and pieces of pigeons. Rage surges. He shouts into the trees, promises death to what did this, and staggers into the woods with a rifle and heavy-toothed traps. With his wife gone—his usual lightning rod—he lets his anger roam. He plants seven traps and waits out a violent storm in the attic, gun trained on the little window. When something pads across the tiles and dips its head inside, he fires. The fox drops, and by morning the sky is rinsed clean.

He cleans the attic, closes the cage for good, and goes to collect the traps. The fields look like ribs where plastic greenhouses were torn open by wind; the mud drags at his boots. Without the hunger to kill, the work is just work. Near sunset, searching for the seventh trap, he spots a cloud of flies by a ditch. A boy lies there, leg clamped in the iron jaws, knee mangled beyond saving. Dead. A younger boy crouches nearby, silent, then bolts when the old man speaks.

Shocked and terrified of what he’s responsible for, the old man follows the boy through the trees to a distant neighbor’s house perched higher than his own. The yard is still; no one repairs storm damage. He hides, thinking only of the trap, the witness, the consequences. Panic makes him cruel in his mind, but when he enters, rake clenched, he does not find a living threat. He finds a man on the floor, dead of a wound to the inner thigh, the blood pooled dark and then trailing away through another door.

The trail fades on a gray carpet in a room hoarded with canned food. He lifts the edge. Beneath is a trapdoor and stairs. He descends into cold, chest-deep water reflecting a hard light. In a concrete room, a little boy sits on the top bunk, gripping bloody scissors. A TV floats. Toys drift. A woman hangs in the water at a strange angle. He touches her arm and finds a short chain and a cuff cutting her wrist. When the basement flooded, there wasn’t enough slack for her to breathe.

He tells the boy to stay, but the child says nothing. The old man climbs out and stands on the porch, the wet air heavy with late summer. Sirens approach through the trees. He does not yell now. He listens, and the woods, for once, do not answer back.
